body.content: What I'm trying to get my head around apt cloud is this, I understand how useful it will be for customers with products in multiple LOBs and I'm all for it, but how is that related to what we really need which is to increase revenue. However, this is a difficult argument since we don't have solid data. A conversation for another time. It is interesting what the board members are thinking about it.
for an apt cloud narrative
All this to put together like 2 bullets in a slide deck

haha here...

 

Here's the situation — the previous Claude thread was working from a different data source (a VMware spreadsheet with 49 client IDs) and comparing it against a dimServices snapshot. You've now given me the authoritative MRCTrend two-snapshot approach, which is what Jorge uses. The numbers are significantly different and several of the skeleton's claims are wrong. Let me lay it out.

The corrected headline (MRCTrend, Online services only):

  Jan 31, 2024 Feb 28, 2026 Delta
Hosting $961,862 $670,003 -$291,860 (-30.3%)
Cloud $8,901 $9,494 +$593 (+6.7%)
Total $970,763 $679,496 -$291,267 (-30.0%)

That's 54 unique clients in Jan 2024, 45 in Feb 2026. Annual impact: ~$3.5M.

Including pending (Provision + Migration + Upgrade/Downgrade): Feb 2026 is $796,416, narrowing the gap to -$174K (-18%).

Customer breakdown — the skeleton was wrong on almost every bucket:

Category Skeleton said Actual
Fully gone 10 clients, $195K 12 clients, $244K
Shrunk 15 clients, -$231K 13 clients, -$131K
Grew 24 clients, +$121K 29 clients, +$84K

The biggest factual error: WeirFoulds LLP was listed as "fully gone" at $41K. They actually grew from $37K to $44K (+$7K, +19%). That's a customer the skeleton had in the churn column who's actually expanding.

The BCOM 2000 "Rocky Linux migration" story is wrong. BCOM still has their ESXi 7.0 vHost online ($2.6K), still has RHEL guest VMs, and added another RHEL VM. There's no Rocky Linux 9 in their Feb 2026 snapshot. They grew from $2.9K to $4.4K. Good story, just not the VMware-exit story the skeleton tells.

What IS real and very compelling for the board:

The vHost-specific picture: $406K/mo → $258K/mo (-$148K, -36%). 20 clients lost all vHosts. Of those 20, only 12 left Aptum entirely — the other 8 dropped vHosts but are still spending $62K/mo on other services. That's the multi-product stickiness argument in real data.

The generation cliff is the actionable story:

  • Pre-5.0 hardware: 7 services, 2 months avg contract remaining — dying
  • Gen 5.0: 46 services, $142K/mo, 6 months avg remaining — this is the migration pipeline
  • Gen 6.0+: 27 services, $82K/mo, 26 months avg — healthy, recently signed

41 ESXi services across 19 clients ($124K/mo) have ≤6 months on contract. That's half the active vHost base about to come up for renewal. Each one is a migrate-or-lose decision.

The real migrations in flight (these are the proof points for the board):

  • Heilind: $41K online + $38K pending — upgrading ESXi 7.0 → ESXi 8.0 on new Dell 650xs hardware
  • Blue Yonder: $19K online + $36K pending — moving to Pro Series 6.0 with ESXi 8.0 (came back after losing all vHosts)
  • HYTECK: $14K online + $20K pending — interesting one: $9.2K of pending is Google Cloud. They're going multi-cloud.
  • Chicken Farmers: $24K online + $13K pending — vHost migration in progress

Want me to rewrite Section 2 of the skeleton with the corrected numbers from this MRCTrend data? The structure is good, the story is right (VMware base is contracting, multi-product customers are sticky, single-product ones churn), but the specific numbers and customer callouts need fixing throughout.

body.content:
MA & Fred, 

Can you please pass this along to your teams?

Quick but important update I want on everyone's radar is that we are not renewing our contract with Navisite (now part of Accenture) when it expires in April.

The short of it is that the quality of their work has been declining for some time. The primary techincal folks have been leaving and being replaced with less skilled resources, and most of the time it's our customers who have to deal with the consequences.

Our Service Desk team has already been handling most database work over the past few months (with fewer issues than when Navisite was involved). We've also added a contractor (Donald) which many of you have already met, who brings deeper expertise than we've ever had access to. Additionally, we're cross-training internally so we can eliminate single points of failure.

I know some of you have been hesitant to sell database-related work because of past support issues. We now own this capability holistically and we've been operating for a few months already.

Data is becoming the center of lots of the customer communications. I want to make sure that we are in a position of strength when it comes to those conversations and are in a position to own the outcomes vs. relying on a 3rd party. 

Thank you,

Can you validate this blurb?

 

2. What's Happening to Our VMware Base Right Now

 

A year ago, our 49 VMware managed hosting customers were generating $935K/mo in total MRC (all services, not just vHosts). Today, including services currently in provision/migration, those same client IDs account for $630K/mo. That's a $306K/mo decline (-33%), or roughly $3.7M/yr in eroded revenue.

 

10 customers fully gone - $195K/mo

  • Centro Internacional de Maiz y Trigo: $43K/mo
  • WeirFoulds LLP: $41K/mo (left explicitly citing price increases)
  • Crealogix MBA Limited: $27K/mo
  • Be the brand experience: $22K/mo
  • Dods Group Limited: $18K/mo
  • StraighterLine: $15K/mo
  • Star One Credit Union: $11K/mo
  • 20-20 Technologies: $10K/mo
  • Blue Cow Software: $4K/mo
  • TAB Products: $4K/mo

15 customers downsized - net $231K/mo reduction

  • HYTECK: $84K -> $20K (-77%). Largest single-customer decline.
  • Premia Solutions: $37K -> $7K (-82%)
  • Blue Yonder: $35K -> $19K (-45%)
  • Combined Metal Industries: $31K -> $17K (-45%)
  • Granite REIT: $26K -> $13K (-52%)
  • Checkout Technology: $24K -> $13K (-43%)
  • Adam Matthew Digital: $32K -> $24K (-24%)
  • Chicken Farmers of Ontario: $21K -> $14K (-31%)

24 customers grew or are growing - net +$121K/mo

  • Leek United Building Society: $38K -> $52K (+36%)
  • Townsquare Media: $103K -> $112K (+9%). They consolidated to one vHost but grew overall services across their dedicated footprint.
  • CITYWAY: $11K -> $17K (+59%)
  • Alaya Care: $7K -> $12K (+86%)
  • RepoSystems.Com: $7K -> $11K (+67%)
  • Currencies Direct: $3K -> $5K (+97%)
  • BCOM 2000: $3K -> $265 online today, but $10K in provision (migrating to Rocky Linux 9 on new hardware - this is a VMware-to-alternative transition happening in real time)
  • Cenovus Energy: $3K -> $2K online today, but $8.5K in provision (new ESXi 7.0/8.0 vHosts being built)
  • These are the customers who committed, expanded their relationship, or are actively investing in new infrastructure with us. This is what "sticky" looks like.

A data visibility problem worth flagging

Our Churn table captured only 15 formally cancelled services totaling $5K/mo across this same period. The actual revenue gap is $306K/mo. That means 98% of the revenue erosion is invisible to our churn tracking system. It's happening through downsizing, non-renewals, and service reductions that don't trigger a formal cancellation. If we're relying on the Churn table to understand VMware revenue health, we're seeing 2% of the picture. This is something we should fix regardless of platform direction.

What's still exposed

  • 22 vHost services across 11 customers are still on ESXi 7.0 (end-of-support since Oct 2025). They have to upgrade or leave. CITYWAY alone is $14K/mo on 7.0.
  • 35 of 80 active vHost services expire within 1 month. Half the remaining VMware base is month-to-month right now.
  • Only 30 of 80 vHost services have more than 12 months on contract.
  • Current VMware vHost revenue: $241K/mo across 32 customers, 80 services, 7 data centers (Toronto, Portsmouth, Atlanta, Herndon, Los Angeles, Miami, London).

What this means

The VMware managed hosting base is contracting, and the pattern is clear: single-product customers (vHost-only, shallow relationship) are the ones leaving or shrinking. Multi-product customers (dedicated servers, networking, managed services layered on top) are growing.

This is exactly the problem Apt Cloud + Aptum IaaS is built to solve. Instead of passing through Broadcom's price increases and watching customers walk, we migrate them to CloudStack/KVM where the licensing overhead is zero, wrap managed services around it, and the multi-product relationship keeps them.

BCOM 2000 is already doing it: moving from VMware to Rocky Linux on new Aptum hardware. That's a $10K/mo customer being retained through a platform transition. This is the template.

The 41 customers still here (plus 3 with active provisioning work) are the migration pipeline. The question is how fast we can move them.
